text:
# Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Construction](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/bouw/index.html)

02/09/2025 • 14 minutes reading

[Albert Jan Swart](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/albert-jan-swart/index.html)

The Dutch infrastructure, including highways, locks and bridges, has been struggling with overdue maintenance for years. In the meantime, revenues from fuel excise duties are coming under pressure because motorists are refueling less and less. The large middle parties GroenLinks-PvdA, CDA, VVD and D66 are pushing for the introduction of a kilometer charge or rush hour charge. Specifically, the next coalition could opt for tolling, which offers important advantages. For example, the government could attract institutional capital through tolls, so that the national debt remains manageable.

## Download the complete analysis

Read all the facts and developments in the analysis 'Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll'.

- [Overdue infrastructure maintenance takes its toll (September 2, 2025)

  838 KB](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/ABN-AMRO-Achterstallig-beheer-informatie-eist-zijn-tol_tcm16-272842.pdf)

## Rising costs, falling revenues

Income from fuel excise duties is under pressure, while the government needs much more budget for operation, maintenance and renovation of the infrastructure. Due to overdue maintenance, the Dutch infrastructure has deteriorated, leading to delays for citizens and companies. If the government does not intervene, delays will increase further, leading to higher costs for entrepreneurs and a further deterioration of the Dutch business climate.

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
The budget for operation, maintenance and renovation of important Dutch roads, waterways and rail has been inadequate for years, according to research by the Court of Audit. Many of the so-called structures such as bridges and locks date from the 1960s and 1970s and are in need of major maintenance or renovation. In 2024, the Court of Audit found that Rijkswaterstaat will lack approximately 15 billion euros for maintenance and renewal of the main road network until 2037. In 2025, the estimated deficit until 2038 has even risen to 20.5 billion euros, on a budget of 22.8 billion. An additional 10.6 billion euros is needed for the waterways alone, on top of the currently planned 10.3 billion. An additional €3.4 billion is needed for dikes and dams, among other things. The railway, which is managed by ProRail, requires an additional 1.8 billion.

The overdue maintenance has major consequences for the Dutch transport sector, among others. For example, inland skippers have to wait longer at the Volkerak locks near Willemstad. These locks, most of which were constructed between 1957 and 1967 as part of the Delta Works, are the largest and busiest lock complex for inland shipping in Europe. Thanks to the Volkerak Locks, inland vessels can pass the Volkerak Dam, thus using the important inland shipping routes between Amsterdam and Germany, and Antwerp and Germany. Approximately 150,000 freight ships pass through the Volkerak locks every year. Normally the locks are in use day and night, but since this summer the locks have been closed between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. Due to wear and tear on a lock door, the Yacht Lock can also only be used to a limited extent, meaning that freight shipping cannot use the lock.

## Tolls gradually abolished in the twentieth century

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
## Tolls gradually abolished in the twentieth century

In 1900, the government abolished tolls on national highways, although from 1933 onwards there was a temporary toll on the Afsluitdijk, which had just been opened; the [proceeds](https://www.omropfryslan.nl/nl/nieuws/1188306/90-jaar-geleden-voor-het-eerst-autos-over-de-afsluitdijk-die-toen-nog-een-tolweg-was) of one guilder per car and one guilder per truck went to the National Crisis Committee for victims of the economic crisis in Europe. Municipalities and provinces continued to charge tolls until the national government bought off the tolls in 1953. Bridge fees are only still charged in Nieuwerbrug aan den Rijn in South Holland. The Nieuwerbruggers have probably been collecting tolls since 1651. Three hundred years later they [did not agree] with the national government (https://bruggenstichting.nl/102-bruggen/bruggen-2011/bruggen-september-2011/153-enige-tolbrug-in-nederland-staat-in-nieuwerbrug-aan-den-rijn) about the redemption price for the bridge and the historic bridge keeper's house with bell tower. In 2025, the rate for passenger cars will be 80 euro cents.

It is not only at the Volkerak locks that inland skippers have to wait a long time. The passing times are also too long at the Kreekrak locks and the Krammer locks, also part of the Delta Works, as Rijkswaterstaat notes in its annual report for 2024. For locks, Rijkswaterstaat aims to ensure that a certain percentage of ships pass the lock within the standard time. On the so-called [Main Transport Axis](https://www.binnenvaartkennis.nl/2021/02/vaarwegenoverzicht-nederland/), a term for the most important waterways from the ports of Amsterdam and Rotterdam to Germany and Belgium, only 64 percent of ships passed within the standard time in 2024, far below the target value of 85 percent.

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
However, Rijkswaterstaat will not only be short of approximately 35 billion euros until 2038, but more money will be needed for infrastructure in the short term. In 2023, then Minister Mark Harbers of Infrastructure and Water Management decided to pause no fewer than seventeen major expansion and new construction projects, partly due to a lack of money. However, the budget has been used for overdue maintenance. As a result, for example, extensions of the Volkerak and Kreekrak locks were not carried out. Projects at a number of important traffic jam bottlenecks on highways were also paused, including the Hoevelaken junction. Postponing these investments will further increase delays, both on waterways and on highways.

The Volkerak locks near Willemstad. These locks, most of which were constructed between 1957 and 1967 as part of the Delta Works, are the largest and busiest lock complex for inland shipping in Europe. Due to overdue maintenance, inland navigation skippers have to wait longer. Photo: Shutterstock

The minister still wanted to continue the projects if there were more budget. In the meantime, however, construction costs have risen excessively, partly due to higher material prices and rising collective labor agreements as a result of high inflation and the tight labor market. The budget shortage has therefore increased further. Rijkswaterstaat is currently preparing 5 to 8 billion euros worth of projects for which no budget is yet available. Because these projects require years of preparation, Rijkswaterstaat has already started with a rough planning.

The increasing slowdown poses a risk to our investment climate. For example, the Netherlands dropped in the IMD World Competitiveness Ranking from number 4 in 2021 to number 10 in 2025. In terms of basic infrastructure, the Netherlands is in 19th place. The Logistics Alliance, a partnership of trade organizations and others, reported in 2022 based on research by Panteia that delays cost the transport sector 1.7 billion euros annually. This damage is likely to continue.

## Lock money, lock money and bridge money

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
## Lock money, lock money and bridge money

Passing locks and bridges is free for inland shipping, at least on the important waterways, partly due to the Mannheim Act of 1868. Most municipalities have abolished the so-called lock or lock fees and bridge fees in recent decades, partly to stimulate tourism. There are exceptions. For example, freight shipping in Haarlem pays a rate of 1.3 euros per meter of ship for opening the bridges over the Spaarne river, with a minimum of 36 euros. In the municipality of Utrecht, ships pay 9 euros in crossing fees for passing locks. A reduced rate applies to emission-free vessels.

## Fuel tax revenues are falling

Costs are therefore exploding, and in the meantime revenues from fuel excise duties are under pressure. This is partly because the government reduced fuel taxes in 2022 due to the energy crisis following the Russian invasion of Ukraine, but that is not the only cause. Due to the rise of more efficient engines and electric vehicles, the consumption of fossil fuels in road traffic is decreasing. Although petrol consumption (with the exception of the corona dip) has increased slightly over the years, diesel consumption has fallen sharply. In 2023, the Dutch drove approximately 1 percent fewer kilometers than in 2018, but the use of fossil fuels in road traffic was as much as 11 percent lower.

A steady decline in fuel tax revenues therefore seems inevitable unless the rate is increased significantly further. The Ministry of Finance therefore expects that total car tax revenues will decrease from 2028, according to the Car Letter from then State Secretary Tjebbe van Oostenbruggen last July. Income is expected to peak around 14.5 billion euros in 2027, before gradually declining (if policy remains unchanged) to 12 billion euros in 2040 (2025 price level).

The new NATO standard, which requires member states to spend 1.5 percent of their gross domestic product (GDP) on matters such as infrastructure, is unlikely to provide any relief, because the Netherlands probably already meets this requirement (see box on page 6). For example, the Dutch government will already invest 2.0 percent of GDP in civil engineering by 2024, according to figures from Statistics Netherlands (CBS).

## Politics in The Hague wants to reform car tax

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
## Politics in The Hague wants to reform car tax

The declining revenues from fuel excise duties will have major consequences for the national budget in the coming cabinet period. Politics in The Hague also recognizes this, and also realizes that more money is needed for infrastructure. The responsible minister, Minister Robert Tieman of Infrastructure and Water Management, is not counting on an extra budget, he said in the House of Representatives at the end of June. The budget shows that “the financial trees do not reach the sky,” he said. The outgoing cabinet is currently looking at "alternative financing", according to Minister Tieman.

To stop the decline in car tax revenues, the government sees roughly three options: increasing revenue from motor vehicle tax (taxing ownership), converting the BPM purchase tax into an ascription tax that also applies to the purchase of second-hand cars, or paying according to use.

In their election manifestos, the middle parties focus on pay-as-you-go. There are different forms of this. Many EU countries use various forms of toll collection, for example by charging tolls on specific routes, for driving over a bridge, or by requiring motorists to purchase a toll vignette in advance. Another form is a rush hour charge, where motorists have to pay to be allowed to drive in the city during rush hours, for example. In London, among others, such a peak hour charge, the 'congestion charge', has been successfully applied since 2003. Other cities also have a rush hour charge, such as Singapore, Stockholm, Milan, Gothenburg and New York.

In the Netherlands, a kilometer charge has been discussed for decades under the heading 'road pricing'. The government is investigating, among other things, a variant in which motorists have to pay annually based on changes in the vehicle's mileage, a good option in terms of privacy. The Truck Tax will come into effect for trucks on July 1, 2026, with payment per kilometer driven on main roads. Depending on the weight and emissions of the vehicle, different rates apply. Cameras record how many kilometers trucks travel.

GroenLinks-PvdA advocates “pay as you go” for motorists, with a lower rate applicable to residents of regions with limited public transport. GroenLinks-PvdA also wants to differentiate according to time. The party also wants to increase the truck tax to fund infrastructure. Elsewhere in the election manifesto, the party says it wants to use "financial buffers", for example for infrastructure spending.

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
The CDA wants a "time and location-related congestion charge", such as a rush hour charge around the major cities. The CDA wants to invest the proceeds in improving the infrastructure. The VVD wants to invest in reliable infrastructure, but at the same time keep fuel taxes low. The party advocates a “future-proof reform” of the car tax, in which working people should not be left behind. D66 will not present its new election manifesto until September 5, but has been advocating for road pricing for years, with a kilometer charge based on the car's CO₂ emissions, location and time.

The four major middle parties are therefore in favor of a reform of car taxes. Three of the four parties are in favor of paying according to use, for example in the form of a kilometer or peak hour tax. Two other parties that are currently doing well in the polls, the PVV and JA21, are against road pricing. JA21 is against both a kilometer charge and a rush hour charge.

One of the forms of alternative financing that the government is investigating is tolls. It is true that the cabinet is “reluctant” when it comes to charging tolls, but in the case of the Blankenburg connection in Rotterdam, the business community around the port of Rotterdam was positive, according to the minister. The minister expects to inform the House of Representatives in the autumn about the different variants for implementation regarding the financing of large, structure-strengthening projects.

Although the minister believes that the infrastructure deficit will not be solved simply, it is clear that significant gaps in the budget could be closed by charging tolls. For example, on the Blankenburg connection, which cost 1.1 billion, tolls amount to approximately 400 million euros. That amount is expected to be raised in a maximum of 25 years.

## The Netherlands may already meet 1.5 percent

At the NATO summit in June, the Netherlands committed to the new NATO standard of 5 percent. According to the new standard, the Netherlands must spend 3.5 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) annually on defense, and another 1.5 percent of GDP on investments in related matters, including infrastructure.

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
Does the new NATO standard offer relief for the neglected Dutch infrastructure, as advocated by the trade association Transport and Logistiek Nederland (TLN)? Presumably not, because the Netherlands may already meet the 1.5 percent standard for, among other things, vital infrastructure. For example, the Dutch government will already invest 2.0 percent of GDP in civil engineering by 2024, according to figures from Statistics Netherlands (CBS). That is more than the 1.5 percent that the Netherlands must spend in a NATO context on infrastructure, among other things.

## Toll collection offers important advantages

There therefore appears to be political support for making motorists pay according to use. Specifically, the next coalition could opt for toll collection, because this offers important advantages. Firstly, tolls have already been successfully applied to the Blankenburg connection in Rotterdam. This example shows that it is not necessary to set up a new executive organization to organize toll collection. Secondly, tolls can make it easier to attract institutional capital. This allows the government to raise money from institutional investors, such as pension funds and insurers, so that large infrastructure projects place much less pressure on the national budget and the national debt can remain manageable.

The Blankenburg connection (A24) near Rotterdam opened at the end of 2024 and is one of the few toll roads in the Netherlands. A rate of 1.51 euros per time applies to passenger cars, and 9.13 euros to trucks. The toll is collected by the Road Traffic Department (RDW). The license plate is photographed and road users can pay via a website, per trip or automatically, or via apps from Flitsmeister, Move-izi and 24toll. Tolls are also likely to be charged on the new extended A15 in the Arnhem region, which will open in 2031. Just like the toll on the A24, the toll on this route is regulated in the Temporary Toll Tax Act on the Blankenburg Connection and the ViA15, which was adopted in 2015.

## Borrow from institutional investors

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
## Borrow from institutional investors

In the Netherlands, new roads were also financed with tolls after the Second World War. [For example, in 1962 the Provincial Council in Zeeland decided (https://www.zeeuwseankers.nl/verhaal/zeelandbrug) to build the Zeeland Bridge (initially: Oosterschelde Bridge), a project costing 77 million guilders (converted to 2025 prices, approximately 300 million euros). The province itself decided to build the bridge because the central government did not agree with the plan. The province set up a limited liability company (NV), which would own the bridge and collect the tolls, and provided it with a loan. The business community also provided loans, worth tens of millions of guilders. Partly thanks to the mediation of the Amsterdam Bank and the Rotterdam Bank (which later merged into Amro Bank), loans could be placed with more than forty institutional investors.

When completed in 1965, the Zeeland Bridge over the Oosterschelde was the longest in Europe at over 5 kilometers. More than forty institutional investors provided loans for construction. Photo: Shutterstock

When completed in 1965, this bridge was the longest in Europe at over 5 kilometers. The toll initially amounted to one euro, but the price was later increased because the number of cars using it was disappointing. Still, the toll probably generated a fairly predictable cash flow, which helped financing. For example, in 1976 the Algemeen Bank Nederland (ABN) provided a current account loan to the NV that the province had established to operate the bridge.

The Blankenburg Connection involved [hundreds of millions](https://www.macquarie.com/us/en/about/news/2018/landmark-blankenburg-connection-ppp-project-reaches-financial-cl.html) investors, such as banks and insurers from Belgium, France, Germany, South Korea and Japan. The extended A15 near Arnhem is [financed](https://www.duravermeer.nl/nieuws/rijkswaterstaat-en-gelregroen-ondertekenen-contract-voor-project-via15) by, among others, John Laing, a British investor who invests in infrastructure for private equity.

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
Charging tolls therefore makes it possible to raise capital from institutional investors, such as pension funds and insurers. The prospect of a stable cash flow from the collected tolls makes financing less risky and therefore reduces interest costs. Indexation of toll rates also protects investors against inflation. By placing infrastructure projects, such as a bridge, in a separate entity, the debt of this entity to banks and institutional investors does not weigh on the balance sheets of governments.

Another advantage of the involvement of market parties is that they generally look more closely at financial risks and costs than the government, write public administration experts Eversdijk and Korsten ([pdf](https://www.arnokorsten.nl/PDF/Samenwerking/Bestuurskundig%20mythe%20van%20onieme%20PPS.pdf)). Rijkswaterstaat could also go a step further by not only allowing market parties to participate in the financing, but also granting the concession to construct and maintain projects themselves. A disadvantage is that most construction companies have become wary of this so-called public-private partnership (PPP), as a result of negative experiences in the past. About ten years ago, several large builders almost went bankrupt because they suffered large losses on complex projects for Rijkswaterstaat where many risks were placed with the contractor in the contracts, so that the contractor had to pay the financial consequences. Nowadays, the number of registrations for tenders for large infrastructure projects is therefore very small. Rijkswaterstaat could make the projects more attractive by taking on more risks, or, for example, by tendering projects in parts, instead of as one large complex whole. In 2025, Rijkswaterstaat decided to divide the maintenance of the Rotterdam Van Brienenoord Bridge into four contracts, after the first tender round in 2024 failed. Only one party had registered, which, according to Rijkswaterstaat, asked for too high an amount.

Charging tolls also offers advantages for road users. This makes it possible to charge different rates depending on the time of day, which can be used to combat traffic jams. The government can offer a discount to certain groups of motorists, such as those living near a toll road or motorists who have difficulty walking and are therefore more dependent on their car, as is the case in London with the rush hour charge.

## The Netherlands must facilitate NATO logistics

metadata.source_title: Overdue infrastructure maintenance is taking its toll

text:
## The Netherlands must facilitate NATO logistics

Additional expenditure on infrastructure seems necessary, also in a NATO context. As a NATO member state, the Netherlands has the obligation to actually deliver logistics performance for defense purposes. Due to its strategic location, the Netherlands is an important transit country for military personnel and equipment. As a NATO member, the Netherlands must then provide so-called '[host nation support](https://www.defensie.nl/onderwerpen/internationale-samenwerking/host-nation-support)', which means that the Netherlands facilitates logistics for "exercises, changes of units or actual deployment". For example, the port of Rotterdam is [regularly](https://www.evofenedex.nl/actualheden/rotterdamse-haven-maak-ruimte-voor-navo-schips) used for the relocation of American troops and equipment to Eastern Europe.

## Conclusion

The shortage of financial resources for the infrastructure of the Netherlands is increasing. Rijkswaterstaat is already short of billions for maintenance and innovation, and will need tens of billions more in the coming years. In the meantime, fuel excise revenues will already come under pressure in the coming cabinet period. The next coalition must intervene to prevent revenues from declining while infrastructure spending increases.

The major middle parties GroenLinks-PvdA, CDA, VVD and D66 are all in favor of a reform of car taxes, for example by introducing a kilometer charge or rush hour charge, possibly on specific roads. For the privacy of motorists, annual payment based on the number of kilometers driven is probably preferable. But charging tolls on specific routes, for example around major cities, is more effective in combating traffic jams. Specific rates may be applied, for example during rush hour or for specific groups of road users.

Another advantage of tolls (or lock fees for inland shipping) is that the government could more easily attract institutional capital to finance large infrastructure projects, without the national debt rising rapidly. It is important that the government applies attractive conditions, so that institutional investors and construction companies are willing to participate.

With thanks to [Bart Banning](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/autoriteit/bart-banning/index.html), [Leontien de Waal](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/autoriteit/leontien-de-waal/index.html) and Bendert Zevenbergen.

## Read more in the construction sector

The Dutch construction sector has been one of the best performing sectors in recent years, despite several challenges facing the sector. Examples are the nitrogen dossier, the significant price increases for energy and building materials and also the personnel shortages. Driven by trends, innovations and legislation and regulations, the relevance of themes such as sustainability and environmental impact is growing and the sector is looking for a new balance.
